main_summary.summary: Judge D'Army Bailey, interviewed by David Cline in Memphis on August 13, 2013, recounts a movement biography spanning Memphis's Crump-era Black politics, Southern University (Baton Rouge) student protests, Clark University in Worcester, Massachusetts, Yale Law School, the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council (LSCRRC), Berkeley California City Council, and the founding of the National Civil Rights Museum at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis. Bailey describes his 1961 expulsion from Southern under Rule 18 ("lack of university adjustment") after the Cox v. Louisiana protests, his role as LSCRRC National Director in 1967 placing law students with Southern civil rights attorneys, his 1971 Berkeley council election with the April Coalition and subsequent recall, and the 1982 foreclosure auction at which he bid $144,000 to save the Lorraine Motel. He details the political fight to secure $8.8 million in state, city, and county funds for the museum, his 1991 resignation from the board after Benjamin L. Hooks replaced him as president, and his critique of corporate capture of civil rights institutions.
key_themes: Foundation funding of civil rights infrastructure
Electoral politics and the April Coalition
Founding of the National Civil Rights Museum
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview provides a rare first-person account of the institutional and financial scaffolding behind the civil rights movement, particularly the role of LSCRRC, the Field Foundation under Leslie W. Dunbar, the Taconic Foundation, the Rockefeller Brothers Fund, and the Norman Foundation in sustaining Southern legal advocacy. Bailey is the primary witness to the December 13, 1982 foreclosure auction at which the Lorraine Motel was preserved for what became the National Civil Rights Museum, and his testimony about the 1991 board takeover by J.R. "Pitt" Hyde III and Benjamin L. Hooks documents a generational shift from movement-veteran leadership to corporate stewardship.

The interview also preserves the procedural mechanics of Cox v. Louisiana (1965) from a participant in the December 1961 Baton Rouge march that produced the case, and contextualizes Berkeley's 1971 April Coalition election (Bailey, Ira Simmons, Loni Hancock) within the post-Ron Dellums opening for Black-left electoral coalitions. Bailey's reflections on Lyndon B. Johnson, the limits of the Voting Rights Act, and the Hegelian dialectic of progress make this a substantive primary source on movement strategy.

chapters.title: Activist Versus Radical
chapters.summary: Bailey opens the interview by distinguishing activists from radicals: the activist accepts institutional parameters; the radical pushes against the outer walls knowing the agenda will not be fully achieved. He discusses the subtitle of his autobiography ("The Education of a Black Radical") and his reluctance to accept labels because they make a person easier to discount and pigeonhole. He also resisted joining CORE during his Baton Rouge years for the same reason.

chapters.title: Defining the Movement
chapters.summary: Bailey describes how he conceives the civil rights movement at the National Civil Rights Museum, beginning with slavery and the abolitionist movement (a framing he credits to designer Benjamin Lawless), and grounded in his own movement experience. He names the Lorraine Motel as the site, references encounters with Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville, the Deacons for Defense in Bogalusa, SNCC freedom singers, Pete Seeger, Carl Braden, and Will D. Campbell. He locates the movement's ignition in the lynching of Emmett Till, the Little Rock Nine, and Daisy Bates with the NAACP rather than in the Brown v. Board decision alone.

chapters.title: Memphis Family and Crump Politics
chapters.summary: Bailey describes his parents, his mother a housekeeper and later licensed practical nurse at St. Joseph's Hospital who became one of the first Black female barbers in Memphis, his father a Pullman porter on Illinois Central from New Orleans to Chicago. He locates his political education in the Shelby County Democratic Club, where as a teenager he worked sound trucks for white moderates and Black candidates including Russell Sugarmon (1959 commissioner race) and Ross Bass. Memphis Black voting was sustained by the residue of the E.H. "Boss" Crump machine even after its mid-1950s decline.

chapters.title: Southern University and Cox v. Louisiana
chapters.summary: Bailey arrives at Southern University in Baton Rouge in 1959, elected Freshman Class President. Following the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Southern students stage lunch counter sit-ins; the resulting case Garner v. Louisiana (1961) establishes that peaceful demonstrators cannot be convicted of disturbing the peace. In December 1961 Bailey marches with Reverend B. Elton Cox around the old state capital; Cox is arrested and the Supreme Court ultimately reverses his conviction in Cox v. Louisiana. Bailey is expelled from Southern in his junior year under Rule 18 ("lack of university adjustment") for speaking at a rally despite warning.

chapters.title: Nonviolence, SNCC, and CORE
chapters.summary: Bailey explains that nonviolence was strategic necessity rather than belief, facing tear gas and dogs, violent response was not viable. After expulsion, Bailey stays at a Black hotel in downtown Baton Rouge, paid for by CORE's national office. SNCC field secretaries Chuck McDew and Bob Zellner arrive to visit jailed organizer Dion Diamond and are themselves arrested for criminal anarchy and sedition. Bailey begins traveling to SNCC staff meetings in Atlanta, meeting John Lewis, Julian Bond, and Jim Forman, while CORE field secretary Dave Dennis grows suspicious of his contact with SNCC.

chapters.title: Clark University and Worcester Student Movement
chapters.summary: Through Walter Williams (a Jackson State student also in the 1961 Wisconsin Seminar), Bailey is offered a scholarship from Clark University in Worcester, Massachusetts, raised by Clark students through car washes and bake sales. He rooms with Fred Jealous (later father of NAACP president Ben Jealous). Bailey founds a Worcester chapter of the Northern Student Movement, picketing Wyman-Gordon, the largest local industrial employer, whose chairman Robert W. Stoddard was national secretary of the John Birch Society. Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination and Defense Department complaints force employment changes. Abbie Hoffman, then in Worcester after Brandeis, publishes the chapter's newsletter The Drum.

chapters.title: Malcolm X and the Deacons for Defense
chapters.summary: Bailey calls Malcolm X at his New York home and persuades him to speak at Clark; when board members demand a counterweight, Malcolm declines to debate Bayard Rustin (whom he calls "a lackey for King"), insisting on debating King directly. Bailey escorts Malcolm through a WTAG radio interview with Julie Chase Fuller. Bailey describes traveling with two priests to Bogalusa in summer 1965, escorted by armed Deacons for Defense and Justice carrying shotguns alongside the march. Bailey distinguishes self-defense (which he accepts) from proactive violence (which he opposes but does not categorically condemn).

chapters.title: Legal Defense Fund and LSCRRC
chapters.summary: Summer 1965 between his first and second years of law school, Bailey works for the NAACP Legal Defense Fund out of Ernest "Dutch" Morial's New Orleans office, filing the first Title VII complaints against Crown Zellerbach in Bogalusa. He transfers to Yale Law School after Dean Jack B. Tate finds him on the waitlist. After Yale, Bailey becomes National Director of the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council (LSCRRC), housed at ACLU's national office, placing 200+ law students with Southern civil rights attorneys at $30/week stipends. Funding flowed from Taconic Foundation, Rockefeller Brothers Fund, Norman Foundation, New York Foundation, Cummins Engine Foundation, and Stern Family Fund.

chapters.title: King Assassination and Move to California
chapters.summary: On April 4, 1968, Bailey is working late in his LSCRRC New York office when his secretary reports the radio bulletin that King has been shot, then dead. The Memphis sanitation strike was the reason ten LSCRRC law students Bailey had placed in Memphis were on the ground, including Alex Hurder, briefly held as a suspect for being a white person near the Lorraine Motel. Bailey flies to Memphis and on to Atlanta for the funeral, then accepts a job at San Francisco Legal Services through former LSCRRC director Steve Antler. Two months in Europe with Europe on $5 a Day precede his October 1968 arrival in California, where he is later fired for challenging the director.
related_events: Martin Luther King Jr. assassination April 4 1968

chapters.title: Field Foundation and Movement Funding
chapters.summary: Bailey details the foundation network sustaining the movement: Leslie W. Dunbar at Field Foundation supports Marian Wright Edelman's Children's Defense Fund and chooses John Lewis over Bailey for the Voter Education Project. Field hires Bailey as a consultant and funds his 1972 black people's community inquiry into Southern University student shootings with $5,000, joined by John Lewis, Owusu Sadaukai, and W. Haywood Burns. A private benefactor establishes the Bread Limited Trust at Bank of America with Bailey controlling $1 million in corpus (up to $100,000 discretionary), with a directive to fund non-tax-exempt projects unable to receive foundation money.

chapters.title: Berkeley April Coalition and Recall
chapters.summary: After Ron Dellums's congressional election opens a Berkeley council seat, Bailey and Ira Simmons declare candidacy through the Berkeley Black Caucus, forcing the April Coalition to accept them alongside Loni Hancock and Rick Brown. Bailey wins on a platform centering Black community interests. Council activity included filibustering meetings, supporting Black Panther Free Breakfast and daycare programs (while refusing Huey Newton's $25,000 funding request), and securing accelerated wage increases for garbage workers during a citywide strike. Mike Wallace interviews Bailey on 60 Minutes pressing him to disclose campaign funding sources; Bailey refuses. After two and a half years, conservatives organize a recall election Bailey loses.

chapters.title: Voting Rights, Mass Incarceration, Obama
chapters.summary: Bailey reflects on Lyndon B. Johnson's leadership securing the Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act, recalling Johnson saying "we shall overcome" in his Southern accent after Selma, Viola Liuzzo, and Reverend James Reeb. Bailey doubts the vote alone produces fundamental change, Memphis Blacks voted under Crump without gaining power. He critiques the Bush v. Gore decision as judicial coup, Citizens United v. FEC as opening corporate ownership of electoral machinery, and Justice Antonin Scalia's "get over it" posture. On mass incarceration, Bailey ties Black imprisonment rates to three centuries of denial that destroyed family structure and self-worth, with gangs filling a vacuum of recognition.

chapters.title: Buying the Lorraine Motel
chapters.summary: Returning to Memphis in 1974, Bailey practices law and writes a weekly Press-Scimitar column for seven years. He meets Walter L. Bailey Sr (no relation), owner of the deteriorating Lorraine Motel, around 1979. After Mr. Bailey's wife dies the night of the assassination, the motel survives on prostitution income and a small Dr. King shrine. Foreclosure proceeds December 13, 1982; Bailey arrives at the courthouse with $65,000, secures $25,000 from union leader Jim Smith minutes before bidding, then secures Tri-State Bank guarantees from Jesse H. Turner Sr through Paul Shapiro and Smith for another $50,000. Bidding against mortgage-holder Raymond Sauer, Bailey wins at $144,000. Jack Belz raises the final $4,000 overnight.

chapters.title: Building the National Civil Rights Museum
chapters.summary: Coretta Scott King's lawyer demands removal of King's name; Bailey complies and renames the foundation the Lorraine Civil Rights Museum Foundation. Designer Benjamin Lawless's $8.8 million proposal, featuring a burned Freedom Riders bus, tear gas effects, and barking dog sounds in the Birmingham exhibit, becomes the museum's enduring design. Bailey secures $4.4 million from the Tennessee legislature (passing 83-17 House, 33-0 Senate after Black Memphis legislators make it their top priority), plus $2.2 million each from Memphis and Shelby County. The McKissack & McKissack Nashville firm is named architect; state architect Mike Fitts manages construction. The museum opens in 1991.

chapters.title: Corporate Capture and Resignation
chapters.summary: Six months after the 1991 opening, Benjamin L. Hooks accepts the board presidency in a 9-6 vote; J.R. "Pitt" Hyde III becomes executive committee chairman. Bailey resigns the same night, having brought Bob Moses, Julian Bond, and John Lewis to opening events with the intent that the museum become a center for renewed activism. He contrasts mid-century foundation funders (Taconic, Field, Rockefeller Brothers), who funded without extracting integrity, with current corporate funders (FedEx, International Paper, AutoZone Inc, First Tennessee Bank). He cites the NAACP's defense of fast food against Michael Bloomberg's portion limits as evidence of corporate-funded movement compromise. Institutions, Bailey concludes, become obsolete or obstacles once they can no longer be used for change.

loc_video.caption: D'Army Bailey oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Memphis, Tennessee, 2013 August 13
